data.h1: Your Unused OpenAI Credits Are Expiring. Here’s How to Sell Them Before They Hit Zero.

data.p: Listen
Share
You stopped using OpenAI three months ago. Switched providers, moved to self-hosted, or simply built differently than planned. The credits are still sitting there. And the expiry date doesn’t care.
Nobody plans to waste AI credits.

They arrived through your accelerator program. Your investor’s platform deal. That enterprise agreement you signed when you were still figuring out your stack. At the time the projections made sense. OpenAI was the right call. The usage estimates felt realistic.
Then production happened.

Your latency requirements couldn’t be met through the API. You found another model performed better on your specific task. Your fundraise consolidated four providers into one. Or you simply built differently than planned — like every AI company does.
The technology decision changed in a week. The credit allocation didn’t.
Now you have an account you don’t use, credits you don’t need, and an expiry date that doesn’t care about any of it.
Every month you wait, the recovery value decreases. After expiry, it hits zero. Permanently.

data.h2: Why This Keeps Happening Across the Ecosystem

data.p: This isn’t carelessness. It’s the default trajectory of AI companies building in 2025 and 2026.

AI startups make infrastructure decisions under conditions that almost guarantee those decisions will change. Choosing providers before production data exists. Estimating compute costs before understanding how token usage actually scales. Architecting systems before knowing what the product looks like six months post-launch.

Standard accelerator cohorts in 2025–2026 included OpenAI credit packages ranging from $10,000 to $30,000 per company. Enterprise partnership programs pushed allocations significantly higher. All issued on fixed 12-month windows. No extensions when plans changed.
The four reasons credits go unused:
Provider switches : Performance data justifying the switch arrives after production testing — after credits are already accumulating unused.
Self-hosted migration : When API costs cross a threshold at scale, self-hosted inference becomes compelling. Enterprise API access becomes irrelevant overnight.
Product pivots : Original use case needed heavy LLM inference. The new direction uses lighter models. The flagship credits stop being relevant.
Post-raise consolidation : Multi-provider seed-stage setup gets rebuilt properly. Some allocations don’t survive.
In every case: technology decisions change in days. Credit allocation sits untouched for months. The asset strands quietly while the clock runs.

data.h2: What’s Actually Sitting in Your Account

data.p: Most founders underestimate what they’re holding.
OpenAI credits aren’t just credits. An account with Tier 5 access is a materially different asset than a Tier 1 account with the same balance.
Here’s why buyers pay a significant premium for Tier 5:
Standard new account (Tier 1) : 500 requests per minute. Fine for development. Insufficient for production AI products at scale.
Tier 5 account : 15,000 RPM, 40 million tokens per minute. Takes 3–4 months of consistent API spending to unlock organically. No application bypasses this. No support ticket fast-tracks it.

For a startup with a production launch in 6 weeks, that 3–4 month timeline is impossible. They need Tier 5 now. Your account already has it. That designation — earned through your usage history — is worth real money to buyers facing hard deadlines.
Four variables determine your recovery value:
Provider and tier : OpenAI Tier 5 carries the highest premium in the current market. Enterprise throughput is the primary value driver.
Credit balance : Face value of unused credits remaining in the account.

Expiry date : The most critical variable. Credits with 10–12 months validity attract buyers at close to face value. Credits with 6–8 weeks remaining attract only urgent buyers at significant discounts.
Time you wait : Every week of delay compresses your recovery window. An asset worth 80% of face value today may be worth 40% in two months and zero in four.

data.h2: This Is Where Credex Comes In

data.p: Credex is a verified marketplace for unused AI and cloud infrastructure credits.
We connect companies holding stranded OpenAI, Anthropic, AWS, GCP, Azure, Cloudflare and other platform credits with companies that need enterprise access now.
Instead of your asset expiring into nothing, it converts to real recovered value. Instead of another team waiting 4 months to unlock Tier 5, they access it in days.
We handle verification, matching, transfer, and payment protection. You submit your details, get a valuation, and decide. No commitment required to find out what your credits are worth.
To understand why your credits sell quickly, understand what buyers are facing right now.
A funded startup ready to launch their production AI product starts at Tier 1: 500 requests per minute.
Reaching Tier 5 requires 3–4 months of consistent API spending. No process bypasses this.

For a startup with an enterprise pilot in 6 weeks, that timeline is a hard wall. They need Tier 5 access immediately. Your account has it earned and ready. That’s not a minor convenience — it’s months of access history they cannot rebuild from scratch.
GPT-5.5 is listed at $5 per million input tokens and $30 per million output tokens. In production, three dynamics change the calculation significantly:

Reasoning tokens : Thinking-enabled models generate internal tokens before producing visible output. A response with 500 visible tokens can carry 2,000+ billed reasoning tokens. Teams expecting $1,500/day find actual bills at $6,000.

Long-context repricing : Above approximately 272,000 input tokens per session, the entire session reprices at 2x input and 1.5x output applied retroactively. Teams projecting $20,000 monthly land at $50,000 after one high-volume week.

Agentic output patterns : Output tokens cost 6x input tokens. Autonomous pipelines produce far more output than simple prompt-response. Teams estimating $8,000/month hit $35,000-$40,000 at real agentic volume.
Buyers facing these cost realities need additional credit runway urgently. Your unused allocation provides exactly that.
OpenAI shipped five major model versions in roughly eight months. GPT-5 through GPT-5.5. Each prior version retires approximately 90 days after its successor launches.

To illustrate without exposing any real client details, here’s a representative scenario based on the type of transactions Credex facilitates:

A B2B AI startup receives $25,000 in OpenAI credits through an accelerator program. Over six months of development they use approximately $7,000 before migrating to self-hosted inference. The remaining $18,000 sits untouched. The account carries Tier 5 status from early heavy usage. Eight months of validity at migration completion.
Four months have passed. Nobody tracks a billing account for a provider they no longer use. Four months of validity remaining. $18,000 heading toward zero.

At the same time a seed-stage AI startup needs Tier 5 access for an enterprise pilot launching in six weeks. Their account is three weeks old. Tier 1. The normal path to Tier 5 is 3–4 months. Their cost projections are already proving optimistic — reasoning models billing at 4x visible output, $12,000 monthly budget trending toward $40,000 at pilot volume.
They need Tier 5 immediately. They need a credit runway. Both are sitting dormant in the first company’s account.
Without a marketplace : $18,000 expires. Zero recovery. The second startup delays their pilot or pays full retail pricing they can’t sustain.

Through Credex : First company submits assets. Matched within 48 hours. Transaction closes in under a week. The first company recovers meaningful value from an asset heading to zero. Second company launches enterprise pilot on Tier 5 access ahead of schedule at 60% below standard pricing.
Both companies built better because the infrastructure moved instead of expiring.
